title: Alangium lamarckii
taxonomy: Alangium lamarckii is a species of flowering plant in the family Cornaceae, commonly known as the cornel or dogwood family. It belongs to the genus Alangium, which is a group of flowering plants native to Asia. The family Cornaceae is part of the order Cornales, a group of flowering plants that also includes the families Alangiiaceae and Hydrangeaceae. Alangium lamarckii is further classified as a species of shrub or small tree, native to the tropical regions of Asia. The classification of Alangium lamarckii reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history within the plant kingdom.
uses: Alangium lamarckii has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's extracts and roots are believed to possess anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties. In some cultures, it is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. Alangium lamarckii is also thought to have potential antiseptic and antiviral effects. Further research is needed to fully understand and confirm its medicinal uses and benefits.

intro: Alangium lamarckii, also known as the Himalayan Alangium or Alangium, is a plant species native to the Himalayan region, belonging to the family Cornaceae. Alangium lamarckii has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic and Unani medicine, and its health benefits include its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which are said to aid in treating various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Alangium lamarckii include alkaloids, glycosides, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Alangium lamarckii is commonly prepared as a decoction, powder, or paste, and is used to treat a range of health issues, including fever, cough, and respiratory problems.
